commands.review_summary:
Restricts docs-auth loopback return origins to development, removes loopback CSP form targets, and adds environment-specific regression tests.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows that current main unconditionally accepts the two loopback origins and the docs-auth page auto-submits the bearer token to the resulting callback.
commands.review_followup: - Keep this member-authored security PR open for the narrow code and specification fixes, contributor runtime proof, and explicit maintainer approval.

target.title: fix: restrict docs auth return origins to dev only
target.body:
## What this fixes

The ClawHub docs auth broker (`/docs/auth`) signs a user in and then **auto-POSTs their ClawHub auth token** to the return origin's callback. The allowlist of return origins included loopback addresses in **every** environment, including production:

```ts
const allowedDocsOrigins = new Set([
  "https://documentation.openclaw.ai",
  "https://docs.openclaw.ai",
  "http://localhost:4173",   // accepted in prod too
  "http://127.0.0.1:4173",
]);
```

In production, a user who lands on `/docs/auth?return_to=http://localhost:4173/...` has their auth token POSTed to that loopback address. On a shared or multi-process machine, any local process bound to `:4173` can receive a signed-in ClawHub bearer token. The production CSP `form-action` also listed those localhost origins, so the browser permitted the cross-origin POST.

This is the `/docs/auth` half of **GHSA-mp85-24m3-52jg** ("Production auth handoff flows can post or redirect bearer tokens to localhost origins").

## Provenance

Both the localhost origin entries and the localhost `form-action` CSP entries were introduced together in `bec93623` ("feat: add ClawHub docs auth broker"). The loopback origins are the docs **dev** server — they were only ever meant for local development but were never gated to it.

## The fix

Gate the loopback origins behind the existing `isDevRuntime()` helper so production only trusts the real docs origins, and remove the now-dead localhost entries from the production CSP `form-action`.

```ts
const PROD_DOCS_ORIGINS = ["https://documentation.openclaw.ai", "https://docs.openclaw.ai"];
const DEV_DOCS_ORIGINS = ["http://localhost:4173", "http://127.0.0.1:4173"];

function allowedDocsOrigins(): Set<string> {
  return isDevRuntime()
    ? new Set([...PROD_DOCS_ORIGINS, ...DEV_DOCS_ORIGINS])
    : new Set(PROD_DOCS_ORIGINS);
}
```

The set is computed at call time so the gate reflects the runtime environment.

## Behavior

| `return_to` origin | Dev | Prod (before) | Prod (after) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `https://docs.openclaw.ai/...` | accepted | accepted | accepted |
| `https://documentation.openclaw.ai/...` | accepted | accepted | accepted |
| `http://localhost:4173/...` | accepted | **accepted** | **rejected** |
| `http://127.0.0.1:4173/...` | accepted | **accepted** | **rejected** |

Local docs development keeps working; production stops handing tokens to loopback. `vercel.json` only applies to Vercel deployments, and the dev server doesn't use it, so trimming localhost from `form-action` has no dev impact.

## Out of scope (follow-up)

The advisory also covers the `/cli/auth` loopback token handoff. That flow legitimately uses loopback redirects in production (standard CLI login), so it can't be dev-gated the same way — hardening it (scoped/short-lived tokens, PKCE or an approval screen) is a larger token-model change tracked separately.

## Verification

- `vitest run src/lib/docsAuth.test.ts src/lib/runtimeEnv.test.ts` — 8 passed (added tests assert prod rejects localhost and still accepts real docs origins; dev still accepts localhost)
- `tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json` — exit 0
- `oxlint --type-aware` on touched files — clean
- `oxfmt --check` on touched files — clean


## Build-verified: production client bundle rejects localhost

The fix hinges on `isDevRuntime()` returning `false` in the production browser bundle (that's the path that auto-POSTs the user's token). Rather than infer this, I ran a production `vite build` and inspected the emitted client chunks.

Compiled `isDevRuntime` (`.output/public/assets/runtimeEnv-*.js`):

```js
function e(v){return typeof v===`string`&&v.trim()||void 0}    // readString
function t(t){if(!(typeof process>`u`))return e({}[t])}        // readProcessEnv — process.env inlined as {}
function a(){let e=t(`NODE_ENV`);return e?e!==`production`:!1}  // isDevRuntime
```

`t("NODE_ENV")` reads from an inlined **empty object** (`{}[t]`), so it always returns `undefined` in the client → `a()` always returns `false`. `import.meta.env` was inlined as `{DEV:!1, MODE:"production", PROD:!0, ...}`, confirming a production build.

Compiled gate (main client chunk):

```js
Xz=[`https://documentation.openclaw.ai`,`https://docs.openclaw.ai`],   // prod origins
Zz=[`http://localhost:4173`,`http://127.0.0.1:4173`];                  // dev origins
function Qz(){return ye()?new Set([...Xz,...Zz]):new Set(Xz)}          // ye() = isDevRuntime → false
```

In the production build `Qz()` (allowedDocsOrigins) resolves to `new Set(Xz)` — **prod origins only**. `normalizeDocsReturnTo("http://localhost:4173/...")` returns `null`. The localhost strings remain in the bundle but are unreachable.

## Defense in depth

Production rejects the localhost token handoff through three independent layers:

1. **Client bundle** — `isDevRuntime()` compiles to a hard `false`; localhost is not in the allowlist (verified by build, above).
2. **SSR path** — `NODE_ENV=production`, same rejection (unit-tested).
3. **CSP backstop** — `form-action` no longer lists localhost, so even if the env gate failed open the browser would block the cross-origin POST and the token never leaves.

The env gate and the CSP both have to fail for a leak, and the CSP failure mode requires a `vercel.json` regression, not a build-env subtlety.
